content.text: By default Claude decides whether to call a tool or respond in plain text. The

parameter overrides that decision: you can force the model to always call a tool, call a specific named tool, or never call any tool. This is essential for structured-output workflows where you need a guaranteed JSON payload rather than a prose response.

attrs.alt: tool_choice options diagram: auto, any, tool, none

content.text: is the idiomatic way to extract structured data from Claude — the model generates a JSON object that matches your schema rather than embedding data inside prose.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# auto (default) — model decides
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[weather_tool],
    tool_choice={"type": "auto"},   # same as omitting tool_choice
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What's 2+2?"}],
)
# → stop_reason: end_turn (model answered without calling the tool)

# any — must call at least one tool from the list
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[weather_tool, search_tool],
    tool_choice={"type": "any"},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What's 2+2?"}],
)
# → stop_reason: tool_use (forced to call one of the tools)

# tool — must call exactly this named tool
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[extract_info_tool],
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "extract_info"},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "John Smith, 42, lives in Boston."}],
)
# → always calls extract_info, regardless of user message content

# none — must NOT call any tool
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[weather_tool],   # tools still listed but cannot be called
    tool_choice={"type": "none"},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in NYC?"}],
)
# → stop_reason: end_turn (text answer only, no tool call)
Structured Extraction with Forced Tool Use
The most common use of forced tool use is extracting structured data. Define a tool whose
describes the data structure you want, then force the model to call it. The

import anthropic, json
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Define the extraction schema as a tool
contact_extractor = {
    "name": "extract_contact",
    "description": "Extract contact information from text.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "full_name":    {"type": "string", "description": "Person's full name"},
            "email":        {"type": "string", "description": "Email address if present"},
            "phone":        {"type": "string", "description": "Phone number if present"},
            "company":      {"type": "string", "description": "Company or organisation"},
            "job_title":    {"type": "string", "description": "Job title or role"},
        },
        "required": ["full_name"],
    },
}

def extract_contact(text: str) -> dict:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        tools=[contact_extractor],
        tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "extract_contact"},  # forced
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    # With forced tool_choice, the first block is always tool_use
    tool_block = next(b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    return tool_block.input  # dict matching the schema

# Usage
result = extract_contact(
    "Hi, I'm Sarah Chen, VP of Engineering at Acme Corp. "
    "Reach me at sarah@acme.io or (415) 555-0192."
)
print(result)
# {'full_name': 'Sarah Chen', 'email': 'sarah@acme.io',
#  'phone': '(415) 555-0192', 'company': 'Acme Corp', 'job_title': 'VP of Engineering'}

Using none to Disable Tools Mid-Loop
In an agent loop you might want to pass tools on research turns but prevent tool calls on the final summarisation turn. Use
on the final call to guarantee a text response:
, the response will always contain a
block. You still need to return a
to continue a multi-turn conversation, but for single-turn extractions you can just read

def force_call_and_extract(prompt: str, tool: dict) -> dict:
    """Single-turn forced extraction — returns the tool input dict."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        tools=[tool],
        tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": tool["name"]},
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    assert response.stop_reason == "tool_use", (
        f"Expected tool_use, got {response.stop_reason}"
    )
    tool_block = next(b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    assert tool_block.name == tool["name"]
    return tool_block.input
documents the contract: forced tool use guarantees a
block. If the API ever returns something else, the assertion surfaces the bug immediately.
Forgetting to return a tool_result in multi-turn loops
: If you force a tool call but don't append a
for the next turn, the API returns a validation error.
Specifying a tool name not in the tools list
causes an API error. The name must match exactly one tool in the
Using tool_choice: none with no tools
array causes an API error — you must supply at least one tool definition even when preventing tool calls.
Expecting a text block alongside a forced tool_use
block — there is no preceding text. Don't try to extract a text block.
Forced tool use scales well to batch extraction — process many documents in parallel, each with its own forced tool call:
the batch respects API rate limits while parallelising extraction. Each future captures its original index so results stay in document order.
work together. The system prompt can provide extraction instructions, context, or examples, while
ensures the output always arrives as a structured tool call rather than prose:
With forced tool use, testing becomes straightforward — you have a structured dict to assert against rather than parsing prose:
These tests are deterministic enough to run in CI — the structured output format means you're testing the extraction contract, not parsing free-form text.
— must call at least one tool.
Forcing a specific tool is the idiomatic pattern for structured data extraction — define a tool whose schema describes the output shape, force-call it, and read
to guarantee a text response on summarisation or explanation turns in an agent loop.
— assert this at runtime to make the contract explicit.
